nativeRange.habitat: Shallow warm-water neritic zones to approximately 2 m depth. Inhabits estuary and lagoon environments, mangrove margins, seagrass beds, sandy backreef flats, and calm reef-adjacent areas. Anchors into soft sandy substrate via buried rhizoidal holdfast; commonly found alongside Udotea and Halimeda in Caribbean sand-substrate communities.

decorPreferences: deep sandy substrate (minimum 5 cm) for rhizoidal holdfast burial
open display area with strong overhead lighting
low-flow or calm zone to prevent uprooting
stable placement away from heavy herbivore grazing areas

avoidWith: urchins that may dislodge or consume the holdfast
large herbivorous fish such as tangs that may graze calcareous algae
strong current or powerhead flow directed at the specimen

summary: A heavily calcified calcareous green macroalga (family Udoteaceae) with a stiff calcified stalk topped by a brush-like capitulum, sand-anchored via buried rhizoids. Native to Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, and wider tropical Atlantic; generally considered notoriously short-lived in captivity (~45 days to a few months). Requires high lighting, stable calcium/magnesium, and a deep sand bed.
careNotes:
Pencil Top (Penicillus capitatus) is a calcareous green macroalga in the family Udoteaceae, order Bryopsidales, class Ulvophyceae — the same genus as Merman's Shaving Brush (P. dumetosus, sw-algae-060) and closely related to Mermaid's Tail (Udotea sp., same family). The thallus consists of a stiff, calcified cylindrical stalk topped by a broad brush-like capitulum (5–8 cm in diameter) of loosely arranged siphons, with mature specimens reaching approximately 5–15 cm in total height. The structure is anchored into soft sandy substrate via buried rhizoids. In the wild it is found in shallow warm-water lagoons, estuary systems, seagrass beds, and sandy backreef areas throughout the Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, Mediterranean, and eastern Australia.

SHORT LIFESPAN — CRITICAL CARE NOTE: Penicillus capitatus is well-documented as semelparous or near-semelparous, with individual thalli reported to live as little as 45 days to approximately 8 weeks per study, with some specimens living nearly twice as long. In captivity, this inherently short lifespan is widely reported as a significant challenge — specimens frequently decline and die within weeks even in well-maintained reef systems. Hobbyists are generally advised to treat specimen death as likely within weeks to a couple of months and not interpret it as a husbandry failure per se; however, optimal conditions may extend the window somewhat. Purchasing multiple specimens and attempting fragmentation propagation is generally advised to maintain a continuous display presence.

SIBLING SPECIES: P. capitatus and P. dumetosus (Merman's Shaving Brush, sw-algae-060) are congeners sharing essentially identical care requirements, the same semelparous life history, and the same sand-anchoring morphology. The principal distinction visible to hobbyists is minor morphological variation in capitulum density; for aquarium purposes both species can be treated identically.

SAND BED REQUIREMENT: A sand bed of at least 5 cm is considered necessary to allow the rhizoidal holdfast to anchor. Without adequate sand depth, the thallus tends to tip over and fail to establish.

CALCIUM AND ALKALINITY: As a calcareous alga depositing CaCO3 (aragonite) into the stalk and portions of the capitulum, P. capitatus benefits from stable reef-grade calcium (380–450 ppm), magnesium (1,250–1,350 ppm), and alkalinity (8–12 dKH). Drops in calcium or magnesium have been reported to accelerate thallus decline.

LIGHTING: High-intensity lighting is generally advised; calcareous green algae in the Udoteaceae are consistently documented as requiring ample PAR to support photosynthesis and active calcification. Positioning under strong overhead lighting is commonly recommended.

FLOW: Low to moderate flow is generally preferred — sufficient for gas exchange but not strong enough to dislodge or stress the sandy substrate anchor.

PALATABILITY: The calcified structure is considered largely unpalatable to most marine fish and invertebrates, giving it reasonable grazing resistance in a community reef. Urchins and some large herbivorous fish may still damage or dislodge specimens.

SOURCE COVERAGE CAVEAT: This entry is based primarily on the Mosaic Macros product page (sole known hobby-trade source at time of writing, 2026-06-08) and the Wikipedia Penicillus capitatus species article, supplemented by family-level care extrapolation from the congeneric Merman's Shaving Brush (sw-algae-060) entry. Species-specific aquarium data for P. capitatus is sparse; water parameters and husbandry values reflect standard reef chemistry applied consistently across calcareous green macroalgae.
breedingNotes: Propagation in captivity is generally attempted by fragmentation — detaching a healthy section of the brush or stalk base and anchoring it into sand, though success rates are variable given the short natural lifespan. Sexual reproduction via gamete dispersal is not a practical captive propagation method. The genus is considered semelparous or near-semelparous; each thallus reproduces once and dies, so maintaining the species in a tank long-term typically requires purchasing new specimens or successfully propagating fragments before the parent thallus declines.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Sole hobby-trade source found for this specialty entry. Product listing confirms scientific name Penicillus capitatus, common name 'Pencil Top', heavily calcified sand-substrate macroalga with brush-like top, low palatability to most marine fish and invertebrates. Minimal care detail on product page.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Penicillus capitatus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy: family Udoteaceae, order Bryopsidales, class Ulvophyceae, division Chlorophyta. Distribution: Gulf of Mexico, Caribbean, Eastern South America, Mediterranean, eastern Australia. Habitat: shallow neritic zones to ~2 m, lagoon/estuary/seagrass/mangrove environments. Morphology: stipe + capitulum (5–8 cm diameter), mature height ~15 cm. Lifespan: ~45 days to ~8 weeks per thallus (semelparous), some specimens reported living nearly twice as long. High-stakes fields cross-checked against congeneric P. dumetosus data in sibling file sw-algae-060.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
